back-office-automation.example: "Once back-office automation entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."

askNext: What stops the agent when it loops or exceeds budget?
Which actions require approval versus silent execution?
Where is the durable trace of what it did while nobody watched?
background-agent.body: Background agents move work into time you are not staring at. They also move risk there.
background-agent.example: "The background agent opened twelve PRs overnight and one production incident."
background-agent.misuse: Marketed as 'set and forget' without budgets, kill switches, audit logs, or clear ownership of side effects. Background does not mean unsupervised forever.
background-agent.practicalMeaning: Require explicit scopes, resource limits, progress traces, and human review points for irreversible actions. Treat background agents like batch jobs with credentials, not magic interns.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: A background agent is an agentic system designed to continue multi-step work outside a live chat session—often writing code, filing tickets, or producing artefacts that surface later via notification,

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Require explicit scopes, resource limits, progress traces, and human review points for irreversible actions.

background-agent.warningLabel: Out of sight is not out of blast radius.
background-agent.whyExists: Useful work often takes longer than a single conversation window. Products needed a shape for long-running delegated tasks without blocking a human at the keyboard.

askNext: What states can the job enter, and how does the user learn about each one?
Can it be cancelled or safely retried without duplicating side effects?
How long are prompts, intermediate state, and results retained?
background-mode.body: The foreground is where the demo happens. The background is where the product learns whether it has a job system.
background-mode.example: "The team enabled background mode, then discovered that 'come back later' still requires somewhere sensible to come back to."
background-mode.misuse: It is described as though putting work in the background makes it durable, observable, or safe. Those properties depend on the provider contract and the application's handling of retries, cancellation, duplicate work, retention, and partial failure.
background-mode.practicalMeaning: The product must store the response identifier, expose progress honestly, recover after restarts, decide how often to poll, handle expiry and failure, and tell the user when the result is ready. It is asynchronous execution, not an exemption from lifecycle design.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start long model work now, keep its identifier, and design the product to handle the answer later.

background-mode.whyExists: Agentic and reasoning workloads can run longer than ordinary HTTP timeouts or interactive user sessions. Background execution gives them a managed lifecycle with status, cancellation, polling, and eventual result retrieval.

askNext: Is the cited benchmark still discriminative?
What contamination controls exist?
Which harder evals tell a different story?
benchmark-saturation.body: Benchmark saturation is grade inflation for models. Find harder exams.
benchmark-saturation.example: "Benchmark saturation turned a two-point gap into a press release."
benchmark-saturation.misuse: Used to dismiss all benchmarks, or ignored while claims still cite saturated sets as proof of general superiority.
benchmark-saturation.practicalMeaning: Prefer harder, fresher, contamination-aware evals; report multiple suites; treat saturated scores as weak evidence.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Benchmark saturation occurs when widely used evaluation sets no longer separate systems usefully because scores cluster near the maximum or become contaminated by training exposure.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Prefer harder, fresher, contamination-aware evals; report multiple suites; treat saturated scores as weak evidence.

benchmark-saturation.warningLabel: A full score can mean the test retired, not that the problem died.
benchmark-saturation.whyExists: Progress made old tests too easy. The field still needs gradients of difficulty for research and marketing claims.

askNext: What exact tasks and failure modes does the benchmark omit?
Was this result reproduced on the actual workflow or only in a curated harness?
What changed for users besides the slide quality?
benchmark-theatre.body: The chart is usually real. The implied conclusion is where the acting starts.
benchmark-theatre.example: "The deck had seven leaderboard slides and no answer to what the system did on a bad day. Classic benchmark theatre."
benchmark-theatre.misuse: By presenting benchmark wins as if they settle questions about usability, cost, robustness, or risk. They do not. They usually settle only the question of who curated the exam.
benchmark-theatre.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a product story with stronger charts than customer evidence, and a team quietly hoping nobody asks what happens outside the test harness.

translations.label: executive meaning
translations.text: A polished score that allows a strategic conclusion to arrive before the evidence does.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Performance signalling that may have only a passing relationship to live use.

benchmark-theatre.whyExists: Because people need compressed ways to compare systems, and benchmarks are useful when they are honest about scope. The trouble starts when a score becomes a substitute for product evidence.

askNext: What selects the winner, and is that selector evaluated?
What is the latency budget at your chosen n?
Does quality plateau before your spend does?
best-of-n.body: Best-of-n is shopping among futures. Shopping needs a good critic.
best-of-n.example: "Best-of-32 fixed the demo and tripled the invoice."
best-of-n.misuse: Treated as free intelligence. Cost scales with n, and a weak verifier can crown confident nonsense.
best-of-n.practicalMeaning: Track quality versus n, latency, and verifier cost. Use early stopping and task-specific verifiers when possible rather than infinite sampling.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Best-of-n is a decoding strategy that samples multiple candidate outputs and selects the best according to a reward model, verifier, or heuristic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Track quality versus n, latency, and verifier cost.

best-of-n.warningLabel: More samples only help if selection is competent.
best-of-n.whyExists: Single samples are noisy. Searching a small set of candidates often improves quality without changing the base model weights.

bing.body: Bing earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
bing.example: "Bing came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
bing.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
bing.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

bing.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Microsoft AI often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: Is this being used for prototyping, internal tooling, or something expected to survive production traffic?
Who owns the code once the initial chat session has done its party trick?
Did the tool reduce real product effort, or just move the hard part to later?
bolt-new.body: The product is most convincing when the task is genuinely small. It becomes theology when people start treating speed of first output as proof of product maturity.
bolt-new.example: "They used bolt.new to launch the prototype in a weekend, then spent the following week learning that a demo and a codebase are not quite the same species."
bolt-new.misuse: By implying that producing a working demo is the same as producing an enduring software system. The jump from promptable prototype to maintainable product remains as real as ever; the product just postpones the moment you notice.
bolt-new.practicalMeaning: In practice, bolt.new usually means faster mock-ups, internal tools, landing pages, and MVP experiments, plus a renewed temptation to confuse visible progress with engineering certainty.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A quicker route from idea to launch page, preferably before prudence gets a vote.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A browser-native AI builder that can accelerate prototypes while leaving the long-term engineering bill intact.

bolt-new.whyExists: Because many people want software outcomes without local setup, framework archaeology, or the social burden of admitting they mainly need a decent prototype by Thursday. Products like bolt.new exist to collapse ideation, scaffolding, editing, and preview into one persuasive loop.

askNext: What actions can the browser agent take without approval?
How are clicks, form fills, and navigation recorded?
What happens when the page changes or asks for authentication?
browser-agent.body: The browser is a hostile API with better typography.
browser-agent.example: "The browser agent completed the form correctly after the team stopped calling the modal a minor detail and started treating it as a state machine with opinions."
browser-agent.misuse: It gets presented as general digital competence. In reality, browser use adds brittle layouts, login flows, pop-ups, state, captchas, permissions, and the ancient risk of clicking the wrong thing.
browser-agent.practicalMeaning: In practice it means screen grounding, action logging, reversible steps, credential handling, rate limits, and careful separation between browsing for information and taking consequential actions.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An agent operating through a browser, where ordinary UI fragility becomes operational risk.

browser-agent.whyExists: Much work still lives behind web interfaces rather than clean APIs. Browser agents offer a way to automate tasks across those surfaces when direct integration is unavailable or too costly.

askNext: Why is browser automation necessary instead of using an API or exported data feed?
How does the system recover when the page layout, login flow, or modal sequence changes?
What guardrails stop the automation from clicking through the wrong account or action?
browser-automation.body: Browser automation is useful when the work genuinely lives in the browser. It becomes a self-own when teams use it to avoid admitting that the integration surface was broken all along.
browser-automation.example: "The browser automation was impressive until the procurement portal added a second confirmation modal and the whole strategy became a memory."
browser-automation.misuse: It gets oversold as if navigating one polished demo path proves durable operational intelligence. In reality, cookie banners, CAPTCHAs, flaky selectors, login policies, and small UI changes remain perfectly capable of ending the revolution before lunch.
browser-automation.practicalMeaning: In practice it means selectors, session handling, retries, screenshots, rate limits, anti-bot defences, and constant suspicion that the page will load something slightly different in production. If AI is involved, add another layer of interpretation and another category of excuses.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A browser script with ambition, retries, and a standing appointment with brittle interfaces.

browser-automation.whyExists: It exists because large portions of modern work still happen inside web interfaces rather than clean APIs. Teams need ways to test products, collect data, and operate third-party systems without hiring someone to spend the week clicking "next".

askNext: Is the task better solved through a browser, an API, or a human-approved export?
How are authentication, recording, proxy use, and data exposure handled?
What happens when the site layout changes or decides the agent looks suspicious?
browser-use.body: The browser remains the world's most successful accidental workflow engine. Browser Use exists because agents have to live in that reality too.
browser-use.example: "Browser Use made the agent useful on the vendor portal, which was exactly the problem and also the beginning of the risk register."
browser-use.misuse: It gets treated as if browser control makes an agent generally capable. Browser automation can unlock workflows, but it also inherits layout drift, auth friction, CAPTCHAs, policy risk, and the old truth that the web was not designed to be calm.
browser-use.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agents can navigate, click, extract, and act inside browser sessions, often with managed infrastructure, proxies, profiles, recordings, and developer APIs around the messy part.

translations.label: agent meaning
translations.text: Let the model operate a browser because the workflow stubbornly lives in a website.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Managed browser automation for agents, with all the web's old fragility now wearing newer shoes.

browser-use.whyExists: It exists because many real workflows still live behind websites, forms, dashboards, and interfaces that do not expose tidy APIs for every task an agent is asked to complete.

askNext: Which provider keys are supported, and which features disappear if we swap one out?
Whose logs, retention settings, and rate limits apply once our key is in the loop?
Are we buying meaningful workflow value or merely renting a prettier console for our own API bill?
byok.body: BYOK is not inherently cynical. It becomes cynical when a product markets itself as full-stack magic while quietly outsourcing the cost basis to your settings page.
byok.example: "The app was cheap right up until we noticed the pricing page quietly assumed BYOK and therefore our own model invoice."
byok.misuse: By presenting it as proof of openness, affordability, or enterprise maturity on its own. Sometimes it is a sensible customer control. Sometimes it is a polite way of admitting the product would rather not carry the inference bill.
byok.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the customer pays the model provider directly, inherits that provider's rate limits and policies, and still pays the application for the workflow wrapped around the key.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: You keep control over the model relationship and we keep our product story.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The expensive dependency is now your problem, though the wrapper still expects applause.

byok.whyExists: Because model access is costly, margins are fragile, and some customers want tighter control over billing, provider choice, and data handling than a bundled plan allows.

askNext: Which features depend on the vendor's default model and degrade once we replace it?
Whose billing, retention, and compliance policies apply after our model is in the loop?
Are we buying real flexibility or merely renting a connector with optimistic documentation?
byom.body: The term sounds liberating because it flatters procurement. The integration work, less flatteringly, still has to happen.
byom.example: "The platform supported BYOM, which turned out to mean we could swap the model and then spend a week rediscovering every hidden assumption in the prompt layer."
byom.misuse: It gets sold as if model portability were clean and universal. In reality prompts, tool schemas, latency, guardrails, rate limits, and output quirks rarely swap over without argument. BYOM often means "bring your own integration work" with better manners.
byom.note: BYOM is often pitched as a clean compatibility feature. The practical value depends on how deeply the product was designed around a single provider's behaviour in the first place.
byom.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the customer keeps more control over model choice, billing, or data handling while the product keeps control over the surrounding workflow. The interesting question is which capabilities survive once the default model leaves the building.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Keep your preferred model and still enjoy our workflow, provided nothing important changes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The customer brings the model; the platform keeps the abstractions and some of the blame.

byom.warningLabel: Different from BYOK: the key is not the only thing moving.
byom.whyExists: Customers already have provider contracts, regional constraints, compliance rules, performance preferences, and strong feelings about paying twice for the same intelligence. Vendors, meanwhile, would still like to sell the interface, orchestration, governance, or workflow layer around the model.
